titleStmt.title: Letter from Fr Francis M. Shaw to Fr Provincial Thomas V. Nolan, 24 November 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Francis Shaw

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Francis M. Shaw (1881-1924) to the Father Provincial Thomas V Nolan (1867-1941) regarding Shaw's recent conversation with Dr Murphy and Dr Dowdall. Francis, or Frank, Shaw SJ was born in Ennis, county Clare. He was ordained on 31 July 1916 at Milltown Park, Dublin. Fr Shaw joined the war early in 1916, working in the 16th General Field Hospital at Le Tréporte. In 1917, possibly due to his nationalistic views, Fr Shaw was dispatched to India, and later to Mesopotamia.Fr. Thomas V. Nolan was the Provincial of the Irish Province of the Society of Jesus (1912-1922) and was a member of the Distribution Committee which looked after the welfare and distribution of the Belgian refugees who arrived in Ireland as a consequence of the First World War.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
24 Nov. 1917. My dear Father Provincial, P.C. I hope to be in Capetown tomorrow, not,
unfortunately, on the way home. I shall be quite an
expert at 'doubling the Cape.' I had an interview on the
way with Dr Murphy, who was consecrated in Dublin
about a year ago: at least I think it was in Dublin.
He was very interested in my Irish news I had to give
him; and in return presented me with two sermons in
French which he had preached in his cathedral.I had
about twenty of his flock on board. I met a sister of
Father Pat Barrett also in one of the convents âSister
Frances, a Loreto nun. She was under the impression that
it was Father Pat who had assissed Fr Willie Doyle at
the end. I did not undeceive her. Dr Murphy is very
well liked. I was told the poor and the children are
his special care. 'The stormy winds do blow,' at present
but one expects that in these latitudes. Our ship is
steady enough as the wind is head on. I am able to
say Mass on board without any difficulty. On this
voyage I have had about seventy in Catholics among
the patients. One of them named O'Keeffe was a servant
at C.W.C during 1908â9. He says he would like to be back
there again. I have given well over fifty Holy
Communions since we left port. One of our Doctors is
Dr Dowdall â his father is or was Chairman of the
Westmeath Co. Council. He is a good fellow. When we
left, Fr OMahony was still at anchor, but I'm sure he
has had an expedition or two since then â he will be
in calmer waters too. I saw that a Father
Fitsgibbon had been wounded. I suppose it is Fr John.
It is a wonder he escaped so long. Fr MacSheahan
will have his work cut out. One of the 'patient â
officers' on board started off with a little attack on
Ireland a day or two ago â I administered two full
hours of information to him on various subjects, as a
consequence â small nationalities, scraps of paper, the
Pope, the 'Priest in politics', the Empire, etc. all came
under review in course of the discussion. I shall be able to write again in a week, so I
can then send my Mass list. I recommend myself to your Reverence, and
everybodys prayers Your Reverence's servant in Christ.
Write to Archbishop's House.Warehouse Rd
as usual FrancesMShaw S.J.
